manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

CLUE: <WORD>
TARGETS: <TARGETS>

Your team words are: air, trouble, club, movie, wife, country, picture, general, dog.
Your opponent's team words are: list, face, song, sex, piece, fire, year, attention.
Distractor words are: system, lot, man, court, reason, boat, state.
The kill words are: stuff.

content.thinking:

Opponent's words: list, face, song, sex, piece, fire, year, attention

I'm exploring other candidates now. "Wild" only works strongly with dog and country. "Small" has similar limitations to "big." "Good" connects to dog, movie, picture, and wife, but that's fewer reliable matches than "big" offers.

I should focus on words with multiple solid connections rather than ones that work with many words loosely. "Military" links to general, air, and dog—three solid connections. "Watch" is promising too: watch movie, watch dog, and watch picture all work naturally, though watch trouble and watch general are weaker. "Moving" and "silent" are too narrow, each connecting to only one or two words effectively.

"War" stands out as particularly strong—it connects cleanly to movie, general, dog, picture, country, air, and trouble. This gives me several reliable pathways across the grid.

I should verify this doesn't accidentally hit the opponent words. War song and war year are possible but weak. The real concern is "state"—"state of war" is a legitimate phrase that could create an unintended connection. War man and war boat don't work well enough to worry about them as distractors.

Let me explore other options. Training gives me dog and general but only 2-3 solid connections. Best is more promising with best picture being an Oscar category, plus best dog and best movie. Open and fresh are weaker, each yielding just one or two reliable links. I'm leaning toward best as a safer alternative that still captures multiple words without the state-of-war risk. picture, movie, country, dog. All form natural compound phrases with "big" — big air is a snowboarding term, big trouble, big picture, big movie, big country, big dog.

But let me also consider "army": it connects to general, dog, wife (there's even a TV show about it), and movie. That's four solid connections, and "army" might be safer than "war" since it's less likely to overlap with opponent words. Checking the distractor list — army song is possible but not particularly common, so the risk seems manageable.

However, "army man" is a real concern since it's both a toy and a phrase, making it a risky distractor. Comparing the two options, "big" gives me six strong, unambiguous connections across all the target words, while "army" has only four and carries more distractor risk. I'm going with big.
